At this chapter begins the account of how the land of Canaan was divided among the tribes of Israel by lot; a narrative showing that God kept the promise he made to the fathers, that this land would be given to Jacob’s descendants. We should not pass over these chapters with difficult names as useless. Where God has a voice to speak and a hand to write, we should find an ear to hear and an eye to read; and may God give us a heart to benefit from it! Joshua was thought to have been about one hundred years old at this time. It is good for those who are old and weakened by age to be reminded of their condition. God considers the needs of his people, and does not want them burdened with work beyond their strength. And all people, especially older people, should make an effort to do what must be done quickly before they die, lest death prevent it. God promised that he would make the Israelites masters of all the countries yet unconquered, even though Joshua was old and unable to do it; old and not likely to live to see it done. Whatever happens to us, and however we may be set aside as despised, broken vessels, God will do his own work in his own time. We must work out our salvation, then God will work in us and with us; we must resist our spiritual enemies, then God will trample them under our feet; we must go forth to our Christian work and warfare, then God will go forth before us.

The land must be divided among the tribes. It is God’s will that every person should know what belongs to them, and not take what belongs to another. The world must be governed, not by force, but by justice. Wherever our home is located, and whatever honest way our portion is assigned, we should consider it as allotted by God; we should be thankful for it and use it as such, while every wise method should be used to prevent disputes about property, both now and in the future. Joshua must be a symbol of Christ, who has not only conquered the gates of hell for us, but has opened to us the gates of heaven, and having purchased the eternal inheritance for all believers, will put them in possession of it. Here is a general description of the country given to the two and a half tribes by Moses. Israel must know their own land and stay within its boundaries; they may not, under the pretense of being God’s special people, take over what belongs to their neighbors. Twice in this chapter it is noted that no inheritance was given to the tribe of Levi: see . Their support must come from all the tribes. The Lord's ministers should show themselves indifferent about worldly interests, and the people should make sure they lack nothing suitable. And happy are those who have the Lord God of Israel for their inheritance, even if little of this world falls to their lot. His provisions will supply their needs, his comfort will support their souls, until they gain heavenly joy and eternal pleasures.
